header.subtitle: A simple binary converter that lets you intuitively play with bits.

bitDisplay.label: Binary Result
bitDisplay.tip: ✨ Tip: Click or swipe across bits to toggle them

width.label: Width
width.tooltip: Choose how many bits to display (8, 16, or 32).

type.label: Type
type.tooltip: Unsigned treats all bits as positive value; Signed uses two's complement.

action.label: Action
action.tooltip: Flip toggles a single bit; Add increments the value by that bit's weight.

errors.tooBig: Input is too big to handle.
errors.tooSmall: Input is too small to handle.
errors.negativeUnsigned: Negative input: Displaying bits as interpreted by the hardware.
errors.tooBigToConvert: Input is too big to display, only took {{bits}} LSB.
errors.tooSmallToConvert: Input is too small to display, only took {{bits}} LSB.

binaryConverter.title: What is a Binary Converter?
binaryConverter.p1: A binary converter is a tool that translates decimal numbers (the base-10 number system we use every day) into binary (base-2), the language of computers. Computers store all information—from text to images—as a series of 0s and 1s, also known as bits.
binaryConverter.p2: Flibit Converter goes beyond basic conversion by letting you interact with individual bits. This visual approach helps students and developers understand how changing a single bit affects the overall value of a number.

twosComplement.title: How Two's Complement Works
twosComplement.p1: In computing, we often need to represent negative numbers. The most common method is Two's Complement. In this system, the Most Significant Bit (MSB) is used as a sign bit.
twosComplement.unsigned: Unsigned: All bits represent positive values (e.g., bits 0–7 in an 8-bit number represent 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128).
twosComplement.signed: Signed: The MSB represents a negative value (e.g., -128 in an 8-bit number), while the other bits remain positive.

bitWidths.title: 8-bit, 16-bit, and 32-bit conversion
bitWidths.p1: The bit-width determines the range of numbers a computer can represent:
bitWidths.32bit: 32-bit: 0 to over 4 billion (unsigned) or -2.1 billion to 2.1 billion (signed).

whyFlibit.title: Why use Flibit Converter?
whyFlibit.p1: Many online converters are static. Flibit was designed for educational exploration. The "Paint-to-Toggle" feature allows you to swipe across multiple bits to see patterns emerge instantly. It's the perfect bit flip tool for debugging low-level code or teaching computer architecture.
